title: Ginger and date scones
subtitle: Warmly spiced scones with stem ginger and sticky dates
excerpt: Finely chopped stem ginger and soft dates give these scones a warmth that works particularly well in autumn and winter. The syrup from the stem ginger jar goes into the milk, adding depth without extra liquid. These are sweet enough to eat without jam. They bake in 12 minutes and the whole process takes under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Ginger scones appear in Scottish and Irish baking tradition from the nineteenth century, where ground ginger was a common spice in domestic baking. Stem ginger in syrup became widely available in Britain from the early twentieth century and is documented in Mrs Beeton's later editions as an ingredient for biscuits and cakes. The combination with dates is a modern British bakery adaptation.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked scones for up to 1 month. Reheat from frozen at 180 degrees C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The cut rounds can be refrigerated overnight and baked the following morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Stem ginger is young ginger root preserved in sugar syrup. The ginger itself is much softer and less fibrous than fresh ginger, making it easy to chop finely so it distributes through dough without creating chewy fibrous pieces. The syrup in the jar is thick and intensely flavoured with ginger oil; a teaspoon added to the dough's liquid gives extra depth without adding extra moisture. Both the ginger pieces and the syrup are distinct ingredients worth using separately.

content.text: Use the ginger from a jar of

as two separate flavour additions. Drain the pieces, chop them finely and fold them into the dough. Stir 1 tsp of the reserved syrup into the milk before adding it. The two together make the ginger flavour more rounded than using ground ginger alone.

Use medjool or other soft, moist dates rather than dried hard dates. Hard dates need to be soaked in hot water for 10 minutes and drained before using; soft dates can be chopped and added directly to the dough.

items.prepNote: from a jar, drained and finely chopped
from the jar, stirred into the milk

content.text: Heat the oven to 210 degrees C fan and place a baking tray inside to heat. Combine the 250 g flour, 1 tsp baking powder, 1 tsp ground ginger and a pinch of salt. Rub in the 50 g cold butter until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in the 40 g soft brown sugar, the chopped stem ginger and the dates. Beat together the 1 egg, 90 ml cold milk and 1 tsp stem ginger syrup. Pour into the flour mixture and mix quickly with a fork until just combined. Do not overwork.

Turn onto a lightly floured surface and pat out to 2.5 cm thick. Cut with a 6 cm round cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the hot baking tray. Brush the tops with milk. Bake for 11 to 14 minutes until risen, deep golden brown on top, and the base sounds hollow when tapped. Transfer to a rack immediately.

items.problem: Dates made the dough sticky and difficult to handle
items.solution: The dates were very moist, or the pieces were too large. Use a sharp knife dipped in flour to chop them into small dice, no larger than 1 cm. Medjool dates are stickier than standard dates; mix them in with floured hands if needed.

The stem ginger syrup step was skipped, or the ground ginger was old. Both contribute different aspects of ginger flavour: ground ginger gives warmth; the stem ginger provides a sharper, fresher note. Use both for the full effect.

The butter softened too much during mixing, or the hot-tray step was skipped. Keep everything as cold as possible and work quickly. A preheated tray is important for rise: the bottom of the scone sets before the sides spread.
